sentences.text: Well, first of all, I could hardly call myself a chemist.
I was the lowliest of practicing-- I was working in a chemical factory.
I was by no stretch a chemist.
But, anyway, Mrs.
Carga, ?] [?
Heta ?] [?
Carga, ?] came to-- decided to come to America.
And so she was really the pivotal person in my life at that.
Point She decided to come to America because her relatives-- her brother was in America.
And other relatives were living in America.
So she and my cousin decided to come together.
My cousin and Mrs.
Carga ?] went into business together.
They became business partners in America.
What year was this?
In, I would say, 1946, give or take a few months.
So they decided to come to America.
So they were the closest people to me who were leaving.
And so I decided that I wanted to go to America.
But I also had a sister in America, and an aunt and uncle who lived in Ohio.
So, in other words, there were relatives I had in America.
And I didn't have any remaining ones in England.
So that's why I came to America.
And so I managed to have my relatives sponsor me to come to America in 1947.
And that's how I came to come to America.
All around me, people were overjoyed.
But I wasn't terribly overjoyed.
I mean, at that point, it became clear to me that I wasn't hearing from my parents.
I didn't know what had happened to them.
I made inquiries to the Red Cross to find out.
And I found out that they had been deported to a death camp-- not a death camp, what was called a work camp in Poland, a place called [?
Itsbitza, ?] in 1942, that all Jews in the village were deported on a particular date in, I think it was July, 1942, and officially declared to be dead at the end of that year.
Now, since the death date was stated as 31st of December, 1942, that sounds like an administrative date.
But, still, they were officially declared dead.
So the reality of that sunk in at that point.
So I was not gloriously overjoyed.
And I was sort of in limbo then, as to what was happening.
So the real attachment I had to living a life was really through Mrs.
Carga. ?]
Well, it was very similar to my parents.
I also made inquiries about my brother, and that he had been first deported to a camp Weterbork, in Holland, and then deported to a death camp in Poland, to Sobibor, that he was declared dead.
And so anyway, these realities became realities.
So at that point, I decided I wanted to come to America, and then I succeeded.
Then when I arrived in America, I arrived by ship.
And I remember the-- seeing the Statue of Liberty, and feeling very apprehensive.
Here's another unknown thing, place, that I'm going to, not knowing what was in store for me.
I knew very little about America, and life in America.
[DRINKS] And, anyway, at the boat, Mrs.
Carga ?] and my cousin, who is also called [?
Heta ?] [?
Heta Carson ?], [?
Heta Carga ?], met me.
And they had rented a room for me fairly near where they were.
They had not established their business yet.
But they had decided to establish a business.
They eventually decided on the kind of a business that was somewhat similar to Mrs.
Carga's, ?] that it was essentially a place where older people retired to in comfort, and some luxury near White Plains, New York.
But, anyway, at that time, they lived in New York.
And they were sort of making arrangements for me.
What they didn't manage to arrange was for me to have a job.
So they had rented this room, and paid for the rent for, I would say, two or three months, I think.
And I had $100 with me.
Incidentally, in England, the official rules were you could only take $10 out of the country.
And I cheated.
I decided I needed more money, because they assumed in England, the organization that handled me, the refugee organization, they assumed that the people in America would give you money.
Well, they were very wrong.
And I knew that.
So, anyway, I came with $100, but no job.
So I started applying for jobs.
And the first place I went to was this sister company of the one I worked with in England, Laroche, Hoffman Laroche in Nutley, New Jersey.
And I had a letter of recommendation from them that I was a good boy.
And so I showed them my letter, and told them if they had a job.
And they said, no, they didn't have a job right now, but they would let me know.
So then I went to other pharmaceutical manufacturers, Merck, and a couple of others, all of them located in roughly the same part of New Jersey.
And they all said the same.
So some months later, I happened to meet a Jewish chemist.
And he told me, they don't hire Jews.
These large pharmaceutical manufacturers, at that time-- this is 1947-- apparently were not employing Jews.
I mean, this has all changed now.
But at that time, they were going to let me know when they have an opening.
And I'm still waiting for the opening, haven't heard from them yet.
Anyway, so my first job was running an elevator.
So what happened then I was walking the streets in New York week after week.
And my money was getting lower and lower.
And I had no money.
And it was getting lower and lower.
And, eventually, when I was down to about $15, I think, I asked my cousin [?
Heta ?] for some money.
I asked her for $10.
And she insisted on giving me 20, and never allowed me to pay it back.
Anyway, I did finally find a job as running an elevator, going up and down an elevator.
And I stayed with that job up until I had enough money to go visit my relatives in Ohio, the ones who had sponsored me.
I didn't plan to stay there.
I just wanted to see them, my aunt.
So I did that probably five, six months after I had arrived in America.
I don't remember the exact date.
So I went there in Dayton, Ohio.
I say Dayton, Ohio.
I used to get letters from there.
And my English landlady would say, there's a letter for you from Ohio.
So anyway, I went there.
My mother's sister, and her husband, and their four children lived in Dayton.
So, anyway, the aunt was very warm.
And what I remember is here, for the first time, somebody embraced me.
It had been eight years, nine years, and somebody held me.
So this-- and embraced me, and cried.
And so she was very warm.
But also, one of her sons, Ted, who was himself a college student at that time, became very influential in my life, in terms of encouraging me to go to college, and being very helpful along the way.
And I might get back into that later.
So anyway, after a couple weeks-- he drove me around to colleges.
And he told me at the time, you should give them the privilege of letting you be a student there.
They are honored by getting you.
So he bucked me up.
So in many ways, he was helpful.
But I went back to New York, and back to the job search, and back to walking the streets.
And my memory of walking the streets of New York were simply horrendous-- not having money, and running out of food, existing on stale bread because it would last longer.
So a main meal for me would be stale bread and milk or something.
And so I was running out of food, and no prospects for a job.
I didn't-- so I was still trying to get a job as a chemist.
I eventually did get jobs, one with a Jewish pharmaceutical company, a very small enterprise.
And later on, I got a-- it didn't work out.
It was not a happy situation there.
Did you tell them about your Holocaust experience?
No, I assumed they knew.
But the-- what had happened-- I won't name the company.
I don't even know whether they exist anymore.
But what happened is that this company, the head of it was Jewish.
And he had had a degree in chemistry.
And he'd been unable to find a job, because he was a Jew.
So what happened then, he set up shop with no experience, really not having the support structure for running a business.
And so that showed up in business practices that were not the best.
So in my particular case, for instance, I got the job through some government employment agency, where they said they would pay you so much a week.
I think it was $20 a week, or 30, something in that range.
Well, I found that at the end of the first week that this included working overtime on Saturday.
What they said was the regular weeks.
So I complained about it.
And they made a big scene, saying, well, we'll raise your salary, and made it the legal amount.
But then some weeks later, they fired me, because I was a troublemaker.
And so the long and the short of it is that this kind of Jewish enterprise existed on the margin of respectability, partly because of the discrimination against Jews in that field.
And so you have a self-fulfilling prophecy.
And I ran into that again in the next job, which was in Dayton, Ohio-- in Cleveland, Ohio, a similar kind of experience.
So, anyway, I thought I was getting back into chemistry through getting work in chemical factories.
But while living in Cleveland, I lived in a student co-op through-- really, through the initiative of my cousin Ted.
He told me about a student co-op in Cleveland.
So I lived there.
Let's go back to New York.
OK.
Well, then I heard about a job through about a job in Cleveland, in the same line of work.
But it was, again, a Jewish company, again where I had similar kinds of difficulties.
And I don't want to go into them.
But it was the same type of thing.
So that's why I went from New York.
But also during New York, I went to night school.
I started night school at City College.
And I was sort of beginning into college preparation courses.
So my first year, I took a two semester course on American history.
The first semester ended with the Civil War.
And the second semester started with the Civil War.
One semester was taught by a black professor.
And one semester was taught by a white professor.
Well, you've guessed it.
It was two totally different wars.
And one was entirely about slavery.
And the other one was entirely about economics.
Anyway, so I started to learn a bit about American society at the college level.
But I had no clear sense of where I was going.
I still thought I would probably become a chemist.
So then, in Cleveland, while I was living in the student housing, I met the person who was a Quaker, and had some contact with a Quaker college in North Carolina.
And he helped arrange for me to get a small scholarship at a Quaker college, Guilford College in North Carolina.
So after staying in Cleveland for about a year, I went to Guilford College in North Carolina for undergraduate school.
And there-- well, I had a small scholarship.
But I also got another small scholarship from a Jewish refugee organization.
And there was another important woman in my life in North Carolina.
Her name was Ada Field.
She lived off the campus.
She was a retired nutrition professor from the University of California at Berkeley.
So she must have been one of the very early woman PhDs in this country.
This was in 1949.
And she was about in her early seventies.
She was retired.
For health reasons, she had retired.
So anyway, living on a farm with her niece.
And I lived at her house.
And in return for working on her farm, I got free room and board.
I and another man lived on her farm for room and board.
No.
So one of the-- I'm glad you brought this up at this point.
After-- well, first of all, [INAUDIBLE] already I was exposed to fairly much of an atheistic attitude, or rather anti-religious attitude, not so much atheism but close to it.
Well-- and then in London, of course, I was with Jewish people.
But after I left England, I became increasingly unaffiliated with everything Jewish.
And I would say for about 25 years, I was totally disconnected from Judaism.
I would occasionally attend Quaker services.
But I didn't intend to become a Quaker.
I practiced no Judaism at all.
And, of course, my parents had been orthodox.
And so I would say it for about at least 25 years, I was totally cutting myself from everything Jewish.
I certainly didn't want to have anything to do with refugees.
And I didn't want to speak German, or have anything to do with Judaism.
In England, in the school, I mean, the transition was not as drastic as it would have been if we'd been in a totally English school, but in English schools I learned English.
And then I went to night school.
But the actual process of learning English is not terribly clear to me.
In one sense, it was made easy by being surrounded by German speaking teachers.
So it was not as traumatic as it could have been.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that had its price.
Not so much why, but how.
I don't know at what point you want to get into that.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I went to Israel much later, in 1972, when I thought I was emigrating to Israel-- I'm jumping ahead now-- I had to confront the matter of learning a language.
OK.
OK.
Yes, I think she knew my story.
So she was-- she herself, then as I said, was a Quaker, and had a PhD.D.
But she was very active in what was then world federalism, of trying to promote a world government, a sane world government.
She was very active.
She was very active in politics.
There were senators-- Sam Ervin, of the Watergate fame, was one of her boys.
She'd tell him what to do.
He would come to her house.
She would tell him how he should run his life.
And he would respectfully listen.
So, I mean, there were many people with whom she kept in personal touch, and bombarded them with letters, and phone calls, and invite them to her house.
And they would come.
She had a very strong personality.
She was a tiny framed little woman, but very strong.
So, anyway, there, too, I was exposed to the idea of making something of yourself.
And so, for instance, she made a lot of herself.
For instance, she was in nutrition.
And at that time, let's say in terms of bread that people eat, white flour was the thing, where she felt the white flour had all the goodness out of it.
So she decided, and made up a formula, for whole wheat flour.
And nobody would manufacture it.

So she set up-- she converted an old barn into a mill, and made her own flour, and got into her Model T, and went around to stores, and sold her flour, simply out of conviction that that's what people should eat.
So she was that kind of a person.
So in terms of my life, after I graduated from Guilford with high grades, I had applied to various universities for graduate school.
By then, I was convinced I wanted to be a sociologist, simply because I liked the subject matter, not because I really had any clear idea of what I wanted to do with it, or how I wanted to use it.
But I was simply intrigued by it, and felt I could do something there in this field.
But I was totally alienated and separate from anything Jewish, and certainly from the Holocaust.
I wasn't thinking about the Holocaust at all, in terms of my life, or contributions anything like that.
But I felt I wanted to go to a sociology graduate school.
So I applied to all the Ivy League schools.
Some turned me down.
But some, notably Harvard, accepted me.
But they didn't offer me any money, any fellowship money.
So I saw no way of going there.
But during-- after I was stuck in that situation, I was still living at Ada Field's house on her farm.
One of the people who came visiting her was the chairman of the sociology department at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, a man named Lee Brooks.
So he heard my story, that I had applied and didn't have money.
I hadn't applied at his university.
It so happened, the University of North Carolina was one of the leading schools in sociology in the country.
It was always among the top 10 in the country.
But I wasn't really aware of this.
But anyways, I met him at her house.
So he told me, we'd be happy to have you.
If you come, we'll try and work something out about money.
But right now, we don't-- we have allocated the fellowships for next year.
So about a month later, I get a letter from the sociology department at Louisiana State University offering me a fellowship.
Lee Brooks had contacted them.
And about a week later, I got a letter from the University of Mississippi offering me a fellowship.
Lee Brooks had contacted.
So I decided, I'll take a chance on Lee Brooks.
And I went to the University of North Carolina.
And they did find money for me.
And I stayed there for my master's and PhD.D.
And it was, in many ways, intellectually the most rich experience I had up to that time, although at Guilford college I also was exposed to very good teachers.
One man in particular, a man named Crownfield, Frederick Crownfield, was a man who had had a PhD in religion, physics, and philosophy from Harvard.
But he was a terrible teacher.
He was not an entertainer.
He couldn't communicate.
But with me, I felt something really clicking with this man.
And I really felt really energized, and connected, to this.
He was, for me, a very powerful intellectual spark, even though in the classroom he was just terrible.
But we'd meet at his home, with seminars at his home.
And so I felt he communicated a love of ideas that was very strong for me.
But, anyway, so at Guilford, there were a number of teachers then who were good teachers, but particularly Fred Crownfield.
Now, unfortunately, he died before I could communicate that to him, that my sense of appreciation for him.
Chapel Hill then was a very different, much more professionally oriented research university.
And there-- I was there from 1952, first for one year.
And then I-- at that time, I decided that I would go in the army for two years to get the GI bill.
And I was in the army for two years, and then came back.
So, financially, it was a little easier, but I also had an assistantship for the rest of the time.
I was in the Infantry School at Fort Benning, most of the time.
Fort Benning is sort of the center of the infantry system in the army, for training infantry officers, and this, and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, first of all, a regimentation was not strange to me, except to my recollection, I never said sir in the two years to anybody.
I don't remember ever saying sir.
And-- so something in me remained independent.
But, anyway, I was an enlisted man.
A finally made it up to corporal.
I was a good boy, in a sense.
But my main job was being a clerk typist in an office.
And so one of the things that I recall from that experience was that we kept records of people who went for refresher courses in the Infantry School.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was-- the German word is-- there's a German word called [GERMAN], being pleased about the misfortune of others.
Well, I was very pleased when high officers came to the office to look at their grades and were very scared about making a poor grade.
As a lowly enlisted man, it gave me joy, which is not a very decent thing to do.
But I do remember it.

Anyway, so-- I mean, actually I'm picking up on that in my recent work, namely that as you go higher and higher in a career, your public image is one of increasing strength, and confidence, and security.
But at the same time, it's possible that your insecurity also grows.
Your vulnerability also grows.
So the guy who is at the first rank of general in the army may be terrified of getting a small bad grade that keeps him from being elevated-- making it to the next hoop.
That is, there may be a very great vulnerability at the very highest level.
And so we have, in a sense, a double track, many of us.
Not everybody necessarily, but a double track of a public confidence increasing confidence, but also increasing fears of vulnerability.
That can also grow, and can be tapped into occasionally.
So, anyway, I'm writing about that currently in something.
But the army experience was eventually-- the main thing I got from the army was the GI bill.
But also, I had taken books along with me from graduate school.
And I had-- I was sort of in the middle of a master's degree program.
And I took heavy books with me.
And I took time to read.
So unlike most of my fellow graduate students, I could devote a lot of time to heavy books, because I had a lot of time.
So in a sense, I got that out of the army too-- the luxury of spending a lot of time with serious books.
So it was sort of a bland affair.
I mean, I totally left out any sort of a personal life.
I didn't have one.
I didn't have-- certainly, no Jewish life.
But I had no kind of personal life while I was in the army.
Then I went back to Chapel Hill in 1955, I believe, and finished the master's.
So at Chapel Hill, I was in a medical sociology training program, where most-- all of us were involved in some kind of ways of bringing social science to bear on the field of health and medicine.
And part of our training was with some psychiatrists-- that is, we jointly did some work with psychiatrists who were in training at the local hospital at the university.
But my particular work was with medical pathologists, physicians who were pathologists.
And my doctoral dissertation was a study of pathologists.
And the way I did that was I stayed with them.
I didn't just interview.
I stayed with them for periods of time from morning to night.
In one case, I stayed at the house, and was around the clock with a particular person, really to study how they worked.
And so that was my dissertation, was on how these physicians related to other physicians.
For instance a pathologist are sort of the policeman of the medical profession.
They are the ones who discovered mistakes.
Did this patient need to die, and so on?
They do the autopsies.
And so I was involved in looking at these people, in terms of how they managed their professional careers.
